PRODUCT_WHITE_BACKGROUND.rationale: Amazon and e-commerce standard: f/16 maximum depth of field across entire product, strobe balanced for pure white background. Over-expose background by 2 stops on separate circuit.
PRODUCT_WHITE_BACKGROUND.proTip: Use two background lights at equal power to blow the background pure white without spill onto the product. Main product light from above at 45-degree angle. Check all four corners of background for grey.
PRODUCT_WHITE_BACKGROUND.commonMistake: Relying on retouching to make the background white — it is far faster and more consistent to nail pure white in-camera with correct lighting ratios.

PRODUCT_LIFESTYLE_NATURAL.rationale: Product in a lifestyle context: natural light, shallow DOF creates warmth and context. Shows the product being used or placed in a real environment rather than isolated.
PRODUCT_LIFESTYLE_NATURAL.proTip: Build a mini lifestyle set that tells a story — the product surrounded by relevant supporting objects at natural proportions. Use window light from the side for depth and warmth.
PRODUCT_LIFESTYLE_NATURAL.commonMistake: Choosing a background that distracts from the product — the product should always be the primary subject. Backgrounds should support, not compete.

PRODUCT_REFLECTIVE_SURFACE.rationale: Jewellery and glass: light tent or sweep diffuser eliminates specular reflections from strobe. The product surface reflects everything — you and the camera must be hidden.
PRODUCT_REFLECTIVE_SURFACE.proTip: Use a light tent or shoot through a hole in a V-flat. Polarising filter over lens and strobe in tandem can eliminate reflections. Work on acrylic mirror surface for controlled reflections.
PRODUCT_REFLECTIVE_SURFACE.commonMistake: Seeing the camera or photographer reflected in the product surface — always check the product for unwanted reflections before shooting. Use a black V-flat with a hole for the lens.

PRODUCT_DARK_DRAMATIC.rationale: Dark background with single controlled strobe creates high-contrast drama that positions product as premium. Common for spirits, fragrances, and luxury goods.
PRODUCT_DARK_DRAMATIC.proTip: A single strip softbox to one side at 70-90 degrees creates a sharp light-to-shadow transition that defines the product shape dramatically. Black cards absorb spill light.
PRODUCT_DARK_DRAMATIC.commonMistake: Using a soft, flat lighting setup on a dark background — without contrast and shadow depth the product appears flat. Hard directional light is essential for this look.

PRODUCT_BEVERAGE_BACKLIT.rationale: Drinks photography from behind creates the backlit glass effect — light passes through the liquid illuminating colour and translucency. Signature technique for spirits and wine.
PRODUCT_BEVERAGE_BACKLIT.proTip: Place the strobe or LED directly behind the glass at the same height. A hair light from above creates rim separation. Use a black background for maximum colour saturation in the glass.
PRODUCT_BEVERAGE_BACKLIT.commonMistake: Placing the light in front of the drink — this shows only the surface, not the glowing translucent quality that makes beverage photography compelling.

PRODUCT_SCALE_COMPARISON.rationale: Size reference shot: maximum DOF to keep both product and scale reference in sharp focus. Consistent lighting and clean background for series shooting.
PRODUCT_SCALE_COMPARISON.proTip: Use a ruler, coin, or common object for scale. Keep camera perfectly level for consistency across a product range. Document all camera and light settings for matched reshoots.
PRODUCT_SCALE_COMPARISON.commonMistake: Changing camera position or height between similar products in a range — inconsistent perspective makes comparison charts look unprofessional.
